Title: Ancient gene clusters govern the initiation of monoterpenoid indole alkaloid biosynthesis and C3 stereochemistry inversion

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Facilitates strictosidine biosynthesis by importing secologanin into the vacuole.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Monoterpenoid indole alkaloids include pharmacologically important compounds such as vinblastine, ajmaline, reserpine, and spirooxindoles.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included as a core common gene because the paper explicitly frames the STR BGC as governing initiation of MIA biosynthesis, although no new functional experiment on MATE1 was performed here.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative genomics/synteny analysis of STR BGC; prior functional role cited in text
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MATE1 is part of the strictosidine synthase biosynthetic gene cluster conserved in Gentianales. 2. It facilitates secologanin import into the vacuole where STR acts. 3. The study's synteny analysis supports ancient assembly of the STR BGC during Gentianales evolution.

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Monoterpenoid indole alkaloids have major medicinal activities.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Located in the reserpine BGC, but no direct metabolic function was experimentally validated in this paper.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. E3UL is a conserved component of the reserpine BGC in Rauvolfia tetraphylla and Catharanthus roseus. 2. Its direct role in MIA metabolism was not tested. 3. It may mark cluster evolution rather than a validated catalytic step.

Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Defined across Gentianales as heteroyohimbine/yohimbine/corynanthe C3-oxidase. Homologs from multiple species were tested; strictosidine was excluded as substrate.
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Branch point / early dedicated step toward 3R MIAs
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidizes 3S heteroyohimbine-, yohimbine-, and corynanthe-type MIAs at C3-N4 to form 3-dehydro iminium intermediates.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq: SRR32911581; SRR32911592; SRR32911600; GenBank sequences: PP911565-PP911588
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: BBE-like flavoprotein oxidase; bicovalent or single His-FAD-linked oxidase depending on homolog
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Identified near YOS in Rauvolfia tetraphylla locus; homologs assayed from multiple Gentianales species
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Rauwolscine; yohimbine; alloyohimbine; ajmalicine; tetrahydroalstonine; corynantheidine; mitragynine
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step for entry into 3R MIA branch
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes formation of 3-dehydro intermediates required for C3 stereochemical inversion and ultimately 3R MIA accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: 3R monoterpenoid indole alkaloids; reserpine-related alkaloids; spirooxindole precursors
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Rauvolfia tetraphylla; Catharanthus roseus; Mitragyna speciosa; Mitragyna parvifolia; Hamelia patens; Gelsemium sempervirens; Ochrosia elliptica
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: In vitro enzyme assays at 30 C; heterologous expression in yeast
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: 3R MIAs include pharmacologically important compounds such as reserpine and spirooxindole-derived alkaloids.
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: GAL-inducible pESC system in Saccharomyces cerevisiae
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous expression in yeast; purified protein assay; LC-MS/MS; UV spectroscopy; NMR; comparative biochemistry across homologs; kinetics; docking/modeling
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Rauvolfia tetraphylla and other Gentianales species tested in yeast/biochemical systems
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HYC3O oxidizes diverse 3S MIAs to 3-dehydro iminium intermediates. 2. This oxidation is the first half-reaction required for C3 stereochemistry inversion toward 3R MIAs. 3. HYC3O homologs are conserved across Gentianales but differ in substrate preference. 4. HYC3O is encoded in a reserpine BGC in R. tetraphylla and C. roseus, with evolutionary links to the GS BGC.
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Rauvolfia tetraphylla and multiple Gentianales species
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Rauvolfia tetraphylla; R. serpentina; Catharanthus roseus; Ochrosia elliptica; Hamelia patens; Mitragyna speciosa; M. parvifolia; Gelsemium sempervirens

Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Canonical and atypical HYC3Rs exist. Some homologs also reduce strictosidine aglycone, indicating dual catalytic activity. Canonical HYC3Rs did not reduce corynanthe-type 3-dehydro intermediates in the tested systems.
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Branch point / dedicated reduction step producing 3R products
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces 3-dehydro MIA iminium intermediates to 3R epimeric MIAs via si-face hydride delivery from NADPH.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May also reduce strictosidine aglycone and broaden product spectra, potentially complicating flux specificity.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: RsCAD7 from Rauvolfia serpentina roots was identified among highly expressed CAD-like reductases; homologs from multiple Gentianales were tested
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step for stereochemical inversion completion
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Generates 3R MIAs such as 3-epirauwolscine from 3-dehydro intermediates, enabling biosynthesis of reserpine-related and spirooxindole-related products.
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Rauvolfia serpentina; Rauvolfia tetraphylla; Catharanthus roseus; Ochrosia elliptica; Hamelia patens; Mitragyna speciosa; Mitragyna parvifolia; Gelsemium sempervirens
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: In vitro NADPH-dependent assays at 30 C; yeast or E. coli expression
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: 3R MIAs include medically important molecules and precursors for spirooxindoles.
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: GAL-inducible pESC system in yeast; bacterial expression for purification
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Candidate screening of RsCAD1-8; in vitro coupled assays; LC-MS/MS; NMR; kinetics; docking/modeling; in vivo yeast strictosidine aglycone reduction tests
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. RsCAD7 was identified as HYC3R by its ability to reduce 3-dehydrorauwolscine to 3-epirauwolscine. 2. HYC3Rs complete C3 stereochemistry inversion initiated by HYC3O. 3. HYC3Rs show lineage-specific divergence, with canonical and atypical forms in different Gentianales families. 4. Several HYC3Rs also reduce strictosidine aglycone, revealing dual catalytic activity.
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Rauvolfia serpentina and multiple Gentianales species
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Rauvolfia serpentina; Catharanthus roseus; Ochrosia elliptica; Hamelia patens; Mitragyna parvifolia; Gelsemium sempervirens

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Yohimbine/reseprine-related scaffold formation from strictosidine aglycone
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: RtYOS is described as the first committed enzyme for reserpine biosynthesis and is colocated with HYC3O in the reserpine BGC.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces strictosidine aglycone to yohimbane-type 3S scaffold intermediates.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Competes with other strictosidine aglycone reductases for shared intermediate.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supplies yohimbine-type 3S precursors for downstream C3 inversion and reserpine biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Reserpine is a pharmacologically important antihypertensive alkaloid.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genome mining and prior functional assignment cited in study; cluster context analysis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. RtYOS resides in the reserpine BGC and is described as the first committed enzyme for reserpine biosynthesis. 2. It provides 3S yohimbine-type scaffolds that can be epimerized by HYC3O/HYC3R. 3. Its colocalization with HYC3O supports coordinated pathway organization.

Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: A GS biosynthetic gene cluster was uncovered by comparative genomics and is interpreted as ancestral relative to the reserpine BGC.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces strictosidine aglycone to geissoschizine/corynanthe-type scaffolds.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Flux allocation among alternative strictosidine aglycone reductases could alter MIA scaffold balance.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes geissoschizine/corynanthe scaffold formation and underlies an ancestral BGC linked to early MIA diversification.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Geissoschizine-derived scaffolds diversify into many medicinal MIAs.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative genomics/synteny; prior pathway knowledge integrated in this study
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Catharanthus roseus; Rauvolfia tetraphylla and other Gentianales in comparative analysis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. A GS BGC was identified and shown to be ancient in Gentianales. 2. The GS cluster likely predates the reserpine BGC. 3. GS supports corynanthe biosynthesis and early MIA diversification.

Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper's evolutionary analysis centers on the STR BGC as the universal initiation module of MIA biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses tryptamine and secologanin to form strictosidine.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Creates strictosidine, the universal precursor of all downstream MIAs including 3R branches.
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Gentianales species including Catharanthus roseus and Rauvolfia tetraphylla
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Strictosidine is the universal precursor feeding medicinal MIA biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative genomics/synteny in this study; prior biochemical evidence cited
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. STR is part of a conserved three-gene BGC with TDC and MATE. 2. The STR BGC likely assembled once in the Gentianales stem lineage. 3. STR produces strictosidine with 3S stereochemistry, establishing the stereochemical starting point for later inversion by HYC3O/HYC3R.
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Catharanthus roseus and other Gentianales species
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Catharanthus roseus and other Gentianales species

Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Part of the conserved STR BGC reconstructed evolutionarily in this paper.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Precursor-supply step; not established as rate-limiting in this paper
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supplies tryptamine for strictosidine biosynthesis and thus all downstream MIAs.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Feeds biosynthesis of diverse medicinal MIAs.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative genomics/synteny in this study; prior biochemical evidence cited
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TDC is a conserved member of the STR BGC. 2. The TDC-MATE framework likely predated STR recruitment in Gentianales evolution. 3. TDC provides tryptamine for strictosidine and all downstream MIAs.
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Catharanthus roseus and other Gentianales species
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Catharanthus roseus and other Gentianales species
